phase1.explanation:
🦫 **Not everything needs to be concurrent.**

**When NOT to use goroutines:**

| Situation | Why |
|-----------|-----|
| Simple tasks | Overhead |
| CPU-bound | Use threads instead |
| Small operations | Not worth it |
| Sequential logic | Keep it simple |

**Go-chan says:**
"Goroutines are great. But not everything needs them."

**When to use goroutines:**
| Situation | Use goroutines |
|-----------|--------------|
| I/O operations | ✅ |
| Parallel tasks | ✅ |
| Background work | ✅ |
| Network requests | ✅ |

**When to avoid goroutines:**
| Situation | Avoid |
|-----------|-------|
| Simple counter | ❌ |
| Single calculation | ❌ |
| Short operations | ❌ |
| Sequential dependencies | ❌ |

**Best practices:**
| Practice | Why |
|----------|-----|
| Measure first | Know if you need it |
| Start simple | Add concurrency later |
| Profile | Find bottlenecks |
| Use sync | Coordinate when needed |
phase1.openingDialogue: When not to use goroutines. Keep it simple. 🦫

phase2.explanation:
Conceptual lesson — no code to break!

**Common misconception:** "More goroutines = faster code."

Actually: Goroutines have overhead. Creating too many can slow things down.

**Common misconception:** "Goroutines always improve performance."

Actually: They add overhead. For simple tasks, the overhead outweighs the benefit.

**Go-chan says:**
"Measure first. Don't add concurrency for the sake of it."

phase3.prompt:
When should you avoid using goroutines?

A) For I/O operations
B) For simple, sequential operations
C) For network requests
D) For parallel tasks

Select the CORRECT answer.
dialogueHints: Think about when concurrency is useful.
